title: Coronation Chicken
subtitle: Poached chicken in a curried mayonnaise dressing with apricot jam and toasted almonds
excerpt: Coronation chicken is cold poached chicken folded into a mild curried mayonnaise. The sauce depends on toasting the curry powder first, which rounds its edges, and using apricot jam rather than sugar for sweetness and body. The dish is made in advance and improves overnight.
sourceNotes: Created by Constance Spry and Rosemary Hume for the coronation banquet of Queen Elizabeth II in 1953. The original recipe, published in The Constance Spry Cookery Book (1956), used a cooked curry sauce base reduced with wine; the simplified cold version with toasted curry powder has become the standard.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make the day before and refrigerate overnight. The flavours improve significantly.
recipe.temperatureNote: Hob only; no oven temperature required.

glossaryTerms.definition: Heating dry spices in a pan with no fat for 30 to 60 seconds until fragrant. The dry heat activates volatile oils, producing a rounder, more integrated flavour. Spices stirred cold into a mayonnaise taste raw; toasted spices blend smoothly.

content.text: Coronation chicken was created in 1953 for the coronation banquet of Queen Elizabeth II by Constance Spry and Rosemary Hume. The original used a more elaborate cooked sauce; the simplified version made with toasted curry powder, mayonnaise, and apricot jam has become the standard. The toasting of the curry powder is the step most often skipped and the step that makes the most difference.

Place the {{chicken-thighs-boneless}} chicken thighs in a large saucepan with the {{onion}} onion and bay leaves. Cover with cold water, season well with salt, and bring to a gentle simmer. Cook for 20 minutes until the chicken is cooked through. Remove and leave to cool completely.
When cool, shred the chicken into generous pieces with your hands or two forks. Set aside.
Heat a small dry frying pan over a medium heat. Add the {{curry-powder}} of curry powder and
for 30 to 60 seconds until fragrant. Tip onto a plate and leave to cool completely.

Mix the {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, {{apricot-jam}} of apricot jam, the cooled curry powder, and the lemon juice in a bowl. Season with black pepper. The dressing should be sweet, mildly spiced, and sharp from the lemon.
Fold the shredded chicken into the dressing until well coated. Refrigerate for at least 1 hour, or overnight. Before serving, scatter the {{almonds-flaked}} of toasted flaked almonds over the top.

items.symptom: Dressing tastes raw and sharp from the curry powder
items.cause: Curry powder was not toasted before adding to the mayonnaise.
items.fix: Toast the curry powder in a dry pan until fragrant and let it cool completely before mixing in. Raw curry powder in a cold dressing has a harsh, metallic edge.

items.cause: Poached too long or at too high a heat.
items.fix: A bare simmer (just occasional bubbles) for exactly 20 minutes gives moist, tender thighs. Check with a fork at the thickest point; the meat should be just white through, no more.

items.cause: Too much apricot jam, or a very sweet jam used.
items.fix: Add more lemon juice to balance. Taste as you mix and adjust before the chicken goes in.

content.text: Coronation chicken was created by Constance Spry and Rosemary Hume of the London Cordon Bleu school for the coronation banquet of Queen Elizabeth II in June 1953. It appeared on the menu as Poulet Reine Elizabeth (Queen Elizabeth Chicken), designed to be made ahead and served cold. The dish became a British buffet staple throughout the second half of the 20th century. The popular version simplified the original's cooked sauce to a straight mayonnaise and curry powder dressing.
